nativeRange.habitat: Rocky shoreline zones of Lake Tanganyika; found among and immediately above rocky substrate at moderate depths, often associated with shell-free rock piles and sediment patches between boulders

decorPreferences: stacked rocks and cave formations to define territories
sandy substrate (fine aragonite or crushed coral to buffer pH)
open sandy areas between rock structures
minimal planting — Vallisneria or Java fern acceptable but not required
flat rock slates for spawning sites

diet.notes: Micropredator in the wild, feeding primarily on small invertebrates, zooplankton, and crustaceans in the water column and substrate. In the aquarium accepts high-quality small cichlid pellets, frozen cyclops, frozen brine shrimp, frozen daphnia, and mysis shrimp. Live foods (baby brine shrimp, micro-worms) are relished and beneficial for conditioning. Vegetable-based or algae-heavy foods should be avoided — this species has a strictly carnivorous digestive system.

goodWith: other Tanganyika rock-dwellers of differing habitat zones (e.g., Julidochromis species, Tropheus if tank is large)
Synodontis catfish (S. multipunctatus, S. petricola) as dither/cleanup fish
Altolamprologus calvus or compressiceps (non-competing rock zone)
Tanganyika shell-dwellers in separate tank zones if space permits
adult conspecifics in established groups of 6+ with adequate territory
avoidWith: aggressive Mbuna cichlids (incompatible water chemistry and aggression mismatch)
small tetras, livebearers, or nano fish (will be predated or harassed)
unrelated cichlids sharing identical territory type
newly introduced conspecifics into an established group without adequate territory

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Neolamprologus pulcher; standard reference photo showing typical coloration.
Wikimedia Commons image showing adult coloration with iridescent blue-white fin margins; good representative photo.

summary: A small Lake Tanganyika cichlid famed for cooperative breeding — non-breeding offspring remain as helpers to defend territory and assist rearing subsequent clutches, one of the best-documented helper-at-nest systems in vertebrates. Multiple locality color forms exist (‛Daffodil', ‛Black Princess'). Requires hard, alkaline water and structured rock territories.
careNotes: Neolamprologus pulcher is endemic to the rocky coastal zones of Lake Tanganyika and must be kept in hard, alkaline water (pH 7.8–9.0, gH 10–20, kH 10–18). Tanganyika buffer salts or crushed coral substrate are commonly used to maintain chemistry stability. Temperature should be kept in the 24–27 °C range; fluctuations beyond this range stress the immune system. A minimum 150 L tank with 100 cm footprint is recommended for a breeding pair; significantly more space (200–300 L+) is needed to maintain a full cooperative group of 6–12 individuals. Aquascape should prioritise dense rock work — stacked stones forming caves and crevices allow the dominant breeding pair and helper fish to establish distinct micro-territories. Fine aragonite sand substrate (5 cm+) provides a buffer for pH and mimics the natural Tanganyika shoreline. N. pulcher is most distinctively kept as a colony: when raised as a group, juveniles naturally remain with their parents and assist in fry defense, territory guarding, and even fanning eggs — a phenomenon that has made this species one of the most intensively studied models in evolutionary biology research on altruism, kin selection, and cooperative behaviour. Dominant breeders may evict or harass lower-ranked helpers; ensure tank length is sufficient for subordinate fish to flee aggression. Multiple locality variants are found in the hobby — ‛Daffodil‛ (N. pulcher ‛Daffodil‛) from Zambia shows striking yellow coloration; the ‛Burundi‛ form is more silver-blue; ‛Black Princess‛ forms have dark melanistic patterning. Note: N. pulcher is closely related to N. brichardi (Fairy Cichlid / Lyretail Cichlid) and the two are sometimes treated as conspecific; N. pulcher (Princess of Burundi) is distinguished by blue spots at the base of the gill cover and on the cheek, and slightly more stocky build compared to the more elongated N. brichardi. Do not mix the two species in the same tank. Conspecific aggression within an established colony is managed through territory structure; introducing new adults into an established group risks severe aggression and should be done with full tank rearrangement.
breedingNotes: N. pulcher is a biparental, substrate-spawning cichlid that forms a stable monogamous pair bond. The breeding pair excavates a spawning site beneath or within a flat rock crevice; the female deposits 50–150 adhesive eggs which are guarded intensely by both parents. Incubation is approximately 2–3 days at 26 °C, with free-swimming fry emerging 7–10 days post-hatch. The species‛ signature trait is cooperative breeding: offspring from previous clutches remain in the group as ‛helpers‛ and actively assist in territory defense and fry protection, a behaviour extensively studied as a model of kin-selected altruism in vertebrates. Helpers gain indirect fitness by protecting siblings and may eventually inherit territory or breed as subordinates. In the aquarium, maintaining the colony intact substantially improves fry survival rates and reduces predation pressure from other tank inhabitants. Brood sizes are modest; multiple clutches per year are typical in well-maintained conditions. Fry are large enough at free-swimming stage to accept baby brine shrimp and micro-pellets. Breeding difficulty is rated moderate due to the need for precise water chemistry and territorial management; spawning itself is straightforward once a bonded pair is established.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Neolamprologus pulcher
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (males to 10 cm, females slightly smaller), temperature 24–27°C, pH 7.8–9.0, hardness 10–20°dH, native range (Lake Tanganyika, widespread), biotope (rocky littoral zone), diet (small invertebrates and zooplankton), cooperative breeding behaviour, taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), and notes on distinction from N. brichardi.

additional.name: FishBase — Neolamprologus pulcher
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 10 cm TL), native range (Lake Tanganyika, Burundi to Zambia), temperature range (23–27°C), diet classification (carnivore/invertivore), taxonomy, and depth range. Confirmed carnivore diet type.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Lake Tanganyika Cichlids
additional.notes: Supplementary hobbyist source; confirmed water parameter requirements (hard alkaline water, pH 7.8–9.0), tank setup recommendations (rock caves, aragonite sand), care level (intermediate), and feeding notes (carnivore, accepts frozen brine shrimp, cyclops, quality pellets). Validated minimum tank size guidance.

additional.name: Cichlid-Forum — Neolamprologus pulcher species profile
additional.notes: Specialist cichlid hobbyist resource; provided cooperative breeding colony management details, locality variant descriptions (Daffodil, Black Princess, Burundi forms), group size and territorial management recommendations, and distinction from N. brichardi. Cross-checked breeding notes and colony dynamics.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Neolamprologus pulcher
additional.notes: UK trade publication species profile; confirmed aquarium size recommendations, decor requirements, diet (carnivore, small frozen foods), lifespan (up to 10 years reported in good conditions), and breeding notes including cooperative helper behaviour.

fish.breedingNotes: Monogamous substrate spawner; 50–150 eggs per clutch, guarded by both parents and colony helpers. Free-swimming fry in ~10 days. Cooperative breeding colony with helper offspring is the natural system; maintaining the group intact greatly improves breeding success. Moderate difficulty due to water chemistry precision and territorial management requirements.
